behaviors.description: Generated function signature matches expected interface (parameter names, types, return type)
behaviors.justification: The real failure — generating code with wrong signature — can be caught by mocking the LLM to return code with incorrect signatures, then testing that signature extraction and comparison logic detects the mismatch. No I/O, real subprocess, or LLM non-determinism is involved; this is parsing and comparison logic. A pure in-process test with mocked code outputs is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM to return function implementations with correct and incorrect signatures; verify that the signature extraction logic correctly parses the generated code and comparison logic correctly accepts/rejects them. Test cases: wrong parameter names, wrong types, wrong return type, extra/missing parameters.

behaviors.description: Generated implementation passes all provided test cases on first or subsequent attempt
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that test execution or test result evaluation breaks, or that the refinement loop doesn't properly re-run tests. Mocking the test runner hides integration issues: we'd test 'call the runner and handle results' but not 'tests actually run correctly.' Real failures only surface when actually invoking pytest or a real test framework against generated Python code — edge cases in test capture, exit codes, failure parsing, and result handling only appear with real infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Create fixtures of generated Python code (correct and incorrect implementations), actually run pytest against them, capture pass/fail results, and verify the service correctly interprets outcomes. Include multiple-attempt scenario: verify that after a failed test run, the service refines the code and re-runs. Do NOT mock the test runner; use real test execution with real test framework.

behaviors.description: Refinement step includes test failure messages in next LLM prompt for learning
behaviors.justification: The core behavior is 'failure messages are included in the next prompt'—this is prompt construction logic, testable at Unit level. Mock the test runner to return a failure message (e.g., AssertionError with a specific message), then verify that the prompt construction code correctly includes that message in the next LLM prompt. MOCK-MISS analysis: a mock with realistic failure formats can expose bugs in prompt assembly, message extraction, or string formatting. Lower levels don't exist.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the test runner to return realistic pytest failure messages; verify that prompt construction code correctly extracts the failure text and includes it in the next LLM system/user prompt. Test edge cases: multiline error messages, special characters, multiple test failures, missing output.

behaviors.description: Service stops after 3 failed attempts and returns structured error with all three attempts and test results
behaviors.justification: This is pure retry logic and data aggregation. Mock the LLM to always generate failing code and the test runner to always return failure; verify the counter correctly stops at 3, the loop exits, and the error response includes all three attempts with their test results. No I/O or subprocess needed. A Unit test with mocks can fully exercise the stop condition and response construction.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM to return code that always fails tests, mock the test runner to always fail; call the service and verify it makes exactly 3 generation-test cycles, then returns a structured error containing all three attempts (code, test results, failure messages). Verify the error response format and completeness.

behaviors.description: Full loop produces working implementation for a function where single LLM call typically fails
behaviors.justification: The real failure is 'refinement doesn't actually help'—this is a claim about real LLM behavior. No mock can test whether a real LLM, when shown test failures, improves its output. Integration-level fixtures showing improvement would pass the test but wouldn't catch the actual failure: that the real LLM doesn't improve in practice, or improves too slowly, or gets stuck. Only a real LLM call—making actual generation-test-refine cycles with a real model seeing real test results—can verify the loop produces working code for hard cases.
behaviors.test_description: Select a function task known to be difficult for a single LLM call (or run an actual single LLM call and observe failure). Run the full generate-test-refine-test-... loop with the real LLM up to 3 attempts; verify the end result is code that passes all tests. This requires real API calls and real LLM non-determinism. Example: implement a complex string algorithm or data structure that a single prompt typically gets wrong.
